title: Great Trail

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Great Trail"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Great+Trail%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Great+Trail%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Great+Trail%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Great+Trail%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Great+Trail%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Great+Trail%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(October 2017)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Great+Trail%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Great+Trail%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Great+Trail%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Great+Trail%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(October 2017)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

(also called the **Great Path**) was a network of [footpaths](/wiki/Trail "Trail") created by [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_languages "Algonquian languages") and [Iroquoian](/wiki/Iroquoian_languages "Iroquoian languages")-speaking indigenous peoples prior to the arrival of [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") [colonists](/wiki/Colonists "Colonists") in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"). It connected the areas of [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") and eastern [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), and the [mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_States "Mid-Atlantic States") regions to each other and to the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") region. Many major highways in the [Northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States") were later constructed to follow the routes established thousands of years ago by Native Americans moving along these trails.

Hinsdale, W. B.; Michigan Schoolmasters' Club. (1929). [*Trade and lines of overland travel of the Michigan Indians*](https://catalog.hathitrust.org/Record/003109224). Ann Arbor, Mich. [?]: [publisher not identified]. pp. [unpaged].

["A Landscape Planning Study of Webster, Massachusetts"](https://web.archive.org/web/20090509210959/http://www.websterlakeassociation.com/Environmental%20Issues/Landsscape%20Planning%20Study%20of%20Webster%20Ma%20by%20U%20Mass.pdf) (PDF). Archived from [the original](http://www.websterlakeassociation.com/Environmental%20Issues/Landsscape%20Planning%20Study%20of%20Webster%20Ma%20by%20U%20Mass.pdf) (PDF) on May 9, 2009. Retrieved December 26, 2006. Conducted by the [University of Massachusetts Amherst](/wiki/University_of_Massachusetts_Amherst "University of Massachusetts Amherst") Department of Landscape Architecture and Regional Planning.
*There’s More Than Rocks, Trees, and Streams In The Woods: An ACQTC Guide for Friends of the Quinnipiac to the Great Trail System of Connecticut*, ACQTC Publications, 1999

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Historic footpath network in America

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Great_Trail "Special:EditPage/Great Trail") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Great Trail"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Great+Trail%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Great+Trail%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Great+Trail%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Great+Trail%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Great+Trail%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Great+Trail%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(October 2017)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/%22The_Great_Trail%22_Marker.jpg/330px-%22The_Great_Trail%22_Marker.jpg)](/wiki/File:%22The_Great_Trail%22_Marker.jpg)

A historical marker along the trail in [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio").

The **Great Trail** (also called the **Great Path**) was a network of [footpaths](/wiki/Trail "Trail") created by [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_languages "Algonquian languages") and [Iroquoian](/wiki/Iroquoian_languages "Iroquoian languages")-speaking indigenous peoples prior to the arrival of [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") [colonists](/wiki/Colonists "Colonists") in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"). It connected the areas of [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") and eastern [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), and the [mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_States "Mid-Atlantic States") regions to each other and to the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") region. Many major highways in the [Northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States") were later constructed to follow the routes established thousands of years ago by Native Americans moving along these trails.

Although some sections of the trail have been called "warpaths", such as the so-called "[Great Indian Warpath](/wiki/Great_Indian_Warpath "Great Indian Warpath")" through [Chillicothe, Ohio](/wiki/Chillicothe,_Ohio "Chillicothe, Ohio"),[[1]](#cite_note-oldeforester.com-1) the primary purposes for these roads was [peaceful](/wiki/Peace "Peace") [trade](/wiki/Trade "Trade"), [hunting](/wiki/Hunting "Hunting"), and gathering of [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resource "Natural resource") along their routes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Some sources describe the Great Trail as beginning at one point or another. However, as there was a gradation between local trails used by few people and more major routes used by many, identifying a point at which the Great Trail begins or ends is an arbitrary matter.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Great Trail system connected with the [Overland Trail](/wiki/Overland_Trail "Overland Trail"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] which led west, as well as other trails to other parts of the [continent](/wiki/Continent "Continent").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

One part of the Great Trail system stretched from [Passamaquoddy](/wiki/Passamaquoddy "Passamaquoddy") territory in northernmost [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") through the [Lakes Region](/wiki/Lakes_Region_(New_Hampshire) "Lakes Region (New Hampshire)") of [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire") and down to the [Shawmut Peninsula](/wiki/Shawmut_Peninsula "Shawmut Peninsula") in [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"). From there it connected to the region of the [Wampanoag](/wiki/Wampanoag_people "Wampanoag people") of [Cape Cod](/wiki/Cape_Cod "Cape Cod"), and over to the territory of the [Nipmuck](/wiki/Nipmuck "Nipmuck") and other tribes around [Lake Chaubunagungamaug](/wiki/Lake_Chaubunagungamaug "Lake Chaubunagungamaug") before connecting to areas of present-day [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") and points farther south.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Another part of the Great Trail system in New England was later followed by [Massachusetts Route 2](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_2 "Massachusetts Route 2"); it leads from [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") to [upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York"). The section now known as the [Mohawk Trail](/wiki/Mohawk_Trail "Mohawk Trail") (used by tribes such as the [Mohawk](/wiki/Mohawk_nation "Mohawk nation") and [Pocomtuc](/wiki/Pocomtuc "Pocomtuc")) leads from the [Connecticut River](/wiki/Connecticut_River "Connecticut River") valley through the [Berkshires](/wiki/Berkshires "Berkshires") and [Mohawk Trail State Forest](/wiki/Mohawk_Trail_State_Forest "Mohawk Trail State Forest") into the area of present-day [Albany, New York](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), the state capital. From here, the Great Trail system connected all parts of the territories where the Five Nations of the [Iroquois Confederacy](/wiki/Iroquois_Confederacy "Iroquois Confederacy") lived.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

In northern New Jersey, the portion of the Great Trail much-used by the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") included choice places to cross the [Passaic River](/wiki/Passaic_River "Passaic River") and to pass through the valleys among the [Watchung Mountains](/wiki/Watchung_Mountains "Watchung Mountains"), notably at [Hobart Gap](/wiki/Hobart_Gap "Hobart Gap"). As the Dutch colonists advanced beyond the proximity of the [Hudson River](/wiki/North_River_(Hudson_River) "North River (Hudson River)"), the new settlers found these paths crucial to their movement. [New Jersey Route 24](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_24 "New Jersey Route 24") generally follows a branch of the trail in this area.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A more southern part of the Great Trail system went from [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") across [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") to [Oldtown, Maryland](/wiki/Oldtown,_Maryland "Oldtown, Maryland"), and then to the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") below present-day [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"). It crossed [Columbiana County](/wiki/Columbiana_County,_Ohio "Columbiana County, Ohio") to [Bolivar](/wiki/Bolivar,_Ohio "Bolivar, Ohio") and [Sandusky](/wiki/Sandusky,_Ohio "Sandusky, Ohio"), and continued west. The part of the Great Trail used by [Colonial American](/wiki/Colonial_America "Colonial America") troops during [Pontiac's Rebellion](/wiki/Pontiac%27s_Rebellion "Pontiac's Rebellion") has been improved as [U.S. Route 23](/wiki/U.S._Route_23 "U.S. Route 23").[[1]](#cite_note-oldeforester.com-1)

As with the [Native Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas")' burning underbrush to clear land for cultivating crops and creating [deer fields](/wiki/Deer_field "Deer field"), the Great Trail shows that the indigenous inhabitants traveled widely on the land, [altering it](/wiki/Land_management "Land management") to serve their needs. These parts of [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") were not an "untouched wilderness," as described by the early colonists.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
